1: kol-hamitzvah (kol-hammitswāh): 'every commandment' — the singular collective noun treats the entire law as one unified demand, not a menu of options. The opening command is tishmeru la'asot ('be careful to do'), the twin verbs of covenant obedience: guarding (shamar) and performing (asah). The purpose clause — 'so that you may live and multiply and take possession' — makes obedience the path to the promised inheritance; the three verbs describe ascending covenantal fullness.
2: anah (ʿānāh): 'to humble, afflict' — the wilderness is here interpreted as intentional divine pedagogy; YHWH afflicted Israel lema'an da'at et-asher bi-levavcha, 'to know what was in your heart.' Nasah (nāsāh): 'to test' — not to acquire information YHWH lacked, but to reveal to Israel itself what its heart contained. The wilderness is the diagnostic chamber of the covenant, not a punishment for Sinai apostasy alone.
3: lo al-halachem levaddo yichyeh ha-adam ki al-kol-motsa pi-YHWH: 'Not by bread alone does man live, but by every word from the mouth of YHWH.' The manna (man) is a word-substitute — it teaches that divine provision flows through divine speech, not natural process. Jesus quotes this verse verbatim (Deut LXX) at his first temptation (Matthew 4:4), redirecting Satan's bread-test to the prior authority of the word that already commands and provides. The motsa ('going forth, utterance') of YHWH's mouth is the creative and sustaining word behind all physical provision.
4: simlathecha lo valah (simlāṯĕkā lōʾ bāletāh): 'your garment did not wear out' — valah means to decay, wear out, become old (the same root in 'vile'); the preservation of clothing alongside manna provision extends the miracle into every dimension of physical existence. The forty-year span frames the miracle: nothing natural survives forty years of wilderness use. The verse stands without elaboration — its brevity is its witness.
5: yasar (yāsar): 'to discipline, instruct, chasten' — the father-son metaphor for the wilderness discipline is the theological key to chapter 8. Ka'asher yeyaser ish et-beno, 'as a man disciplines his son' — paternal discipline is the interpretive framework for Exodus hardship. Proverbs 3:11-12 develops this directly (musar YHWH beni al-tima'as, 'do not despise the LORD's discipline'); Hebrews 12:5-11 quotes Proverbs 3:11-12 and applies it to Christian suffering, completing the typological arc that begins here.
6: lalechet bidrachav (lāleket bidrākāyw): 'to walk in his ways' — the walk-metaphor (halakh) is Deuteronomy's primary ethics image; the covenant life is conceived as a path to travel, not a set of rules to memorize. Three covenant acts are specified: keeping (shamar), walking (halakh), fearing (yare') — cognitive, behavioral, and affective dimensions of the covenant relationship.
7: eretz tovah (ʾereṣ ṭôbāh): 'a good land' — the threefold repetition of tov in Deuteronomy 8:7-10 (good land, v7; you eat and are satisfied, v10; the good land, v10) echoes Genesis 1's creation declaration. The land is described through its water systems: nachalei mayim (streams of water), ayanot (springs), tehomot (deeps/underground sources) — a complete hydrological portrait. The eschatological land of promise is paradigmatically a well-watered garden.
8: The seven species — wheat, barley, vines, figs, pomegranates, olive oil, and honey — constitute Israel's agricultural canon. These are not random but are the covenantally significant products of the land; the later agricultural festivals (firstfruits, Pentecost, Tabernacles) are organized around them. Devash (dĕbaš): 'honey' — may be date-honey rather than bee-honey; in any case, the sweetness principle: the land's produce is not merely adequate but pleasurable.
9: lo techsar kol-ba (lōʾ teḥsar kol-bāh): 'you will lack nothing in it' — the categorical sufficiency of the land; nothing is missing. The geological detail — rocks containing iron (barzel), hills yielding copper (nechoshet) — specifies material abundance in a pre-industrial economy where metal was the index of civilizational power. The Promised Land is both agriculturally and industrially complete.
10: berakhta et-YHWH Eloheicha (ûbēraktā ʾeṯ-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'you shall bless the LORD your God' — the command to bless YHWH after eating (ve-akhalta ve-savata) is the rabbinic basis for the Birkat Hamazon (grace after meals), considered a Toraitic obligation. The sequence — eat, be satisfied, bless — reverses the anxiety sequence of hoarding: you may eat fully because the giver is reliable, and the proper response to satiation is gratitude rather than expanded appetite.
11: pen tishkach et-YHWH Eloheicha (pen-tiškaḥ ʾeṯ-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'beware lest you forget the LORD your God' — the forget/remember axis is the backbone of Deuteronomy. Zakhor (remember) and shakhach (forget) are the two poles of covenant faithfulness and failure. Forgetting YHWH is defined specifically as failing to keep the commandments (lishmore mitzvotav u-mishpatav ve-chuqqotav) — it is not primarily an intellectual lapse but a behavioral drift.
12: The prosperity spiral begins: eating to the full, building fine houses, settling. Batim tovim (bāttîm ṭôbîm): 'fine houses' — the same tov (good/fine) that characterized the land now describes human constructions; the danger is when man's productions rival the land's gift. The five verbs (ate, built, settled, increased, multiplied, v13) trace the arc of prosperity that threatens covenant memory.
13: Material expansion continues — the triad of flock, silver, gold — the classic markers of patriarchal prosperity (cf. Genesis 13:2, Abraham). Kesef ve-zahav (kesep wĕzāhāb): 'silver and gold' — the two monetary metals that summarize wealth. The expansion is not condemned; prosperity is a covenant blessing. The danger lies not in the wealth but in the heart-response it generates (v14).
14: veram levavecha (wĕrām lĕbābĕkā): 'your heart will become proud/lifted up' — rum is elevation; pride is the heart lifting itself above its proper creaturely position. The result is forgetting YHWH (vishkachta et-YHWH Eloheicha). The anatomy of apostasy: prosperity → proud heart → forgetting → idolatry/disobedience. The identification of Egypt as beit avadim (house of slaves) reminds that the prosperity was not earned but given from a state of utter dependency.
15: nachash saraph (nāḥāš śārāp): 'venomous/fiery serpent' — saraph means burning, fiery; probably describing the burning sensation of the venom (cf. Numbers 21:6-9, the bronze serpent). The wilderness is not merely difficult but actively hostile — snakes, scorpions, thirst, no water. Against this backdrop, the flint-water miracle (hamotz'i lecha mayim mi-tsur hachalamish) is the measure of YHWH's transforming provision: water from the hardest rock.
16: The manna returns as interpretive summary: its purpose was le'anothecha u-lensasothecha ('to humble and test you'), leheitiv lecha be'acharitecha ('to do you good in the end'). The wilderness suffering is teleological — oriented toward a good end that the sufferer cannot see in the moment. This is the theodicy of Deuteronomy 8: affliction serves formation, formation serves inheritance.
17: kochi ve'otsem yadi asah li et-hachayil hazeh (kōḥî wĕʿōṣem yādî ʿāśāh lî ʾeṯ-haḥayil hazzeh): 'My power and the strength of my hands produced this wealth for me' — the formula of self-sufficiency; the precise inversion of covenant acknowledgment. Chayil means both wealth and valor/strength; the self-made-man ideology claims that human chayil generates its own chayil. This verse is the theological ground of every false gospel of self-reliance.
18: koach la'asot chayil (kōaḥ laʿăśôṯ ḥāyil): 'ability to produce wealth' — the corrective: productive capacity itself is YHWH's gift. The purpose is specified: lema'an hakim et-brito, 'in order to confirm his covenant' — the covenant, not human ambition, is the framework within which prosperity occurs. What looks like human achievement is YHWH keeping his Abrahamic promise; wealth as covenant faithfulness demonstration.
19: avod tovedun (ʾābōd tōbēdûn): 'you will certainly perish' — the absolute infinitive + finite verb construction is Hebrew's device for emphasis and certainty. The apostasy chain: forget YHWH → follow other gods → serve them → bow down to them — four steps of progressive idolatrous commitment. Each step deepens the covenant breach; the terminus is national death. The ha'idoti vakhem hayom ('I solemnly warn you today') invokes the treaty-witness formula.
20: The nations-parallel closes the chapter: Israel will perish ka-goyim (like the nations) — not as a special covenant people but as just another failed kingdom — asher YHWH mavid mipneihem (which YHWH is destroying before you). The nations are destroyed for their covenant violations with the created order; Israel can be destroyed for its covenant violation with YHWH. Covenant privilege does not exempt from covenant accountability.
